The magnitude of magnetic field at a point at a distance x from the centre along axis of loop is

Baxis =μ∘iR22R2+x232

subject: Physics
solution:
As l2=25 cm2, then l=5 cm=0.05 m.

Given: t=1 s

Velocity of the square loop, v=0.051=0.05 m s-1.

Induced current,

i=VR=BlvR=40×0.05×0.0510=0.01 A

Now force acting on the side of the square loop, F=Bil=40×0.01×0.05

⇒F=0.02 N

Therefore, work done

W=Fl=0.02×l=0.02×0.05

⇒W=1×10-3 J

subject: Physics

solution:
An attractive force will act normal to  X towards Y because the direction of the currents in the wires is the same.

Force exerted on length l due to the magnetic field is given as F=FXY=FYX=I1l1 B12

⇒F=μ0I1I22πrl1

Given here, I1=2 A, I2= 3 A, l1=5 m, r=5 cm.

Putting the values, we have

F=4π×10-7×2×32π×5×10-2×50×10-2

=1.2×10-5 N directed towards wire X.

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic intensity H is defined as the product of the number of turns per unit length in a coil n and the current that it carries I.

The magnetic field of a solenoid is given by B=μ0ni

H=Bμ0=μ0niμ0⇒H=ni   ...(i) 

The given data is 

H=1.6×103 A m-1

n=8 per cm

Substituting the values in equation (i)

⇒1.6×1000=81100×i

⇒i=2 A

subject: Physics
solution:
$\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{di}}{2 \pi \mathrm{R}}[-\cos \theta \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\sin \theta \hat{\mathbf{j}}]$ $\mathrm{di}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\pi \mathrm{R}} \mathrm{Rd} \theta$ $=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\pi} \mathrm{d} \theta$ $\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi^2 \mathrm{R}}(-\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{j}})$
$\vec{B}=-\frac{\mu_0 I}{\pi^2 R} \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Magnetic field on the centre of a circular current coil is given by B=Nμ0i2R, here, N is number of turns, i is current and R is radius of loop.

Now, for, N=2, magnetic field, B1=N1μ0i2R1

For N2=5

Radius of coil =R2=N1×R1 N2

Magnetic field, B2=N2μ0i2R2

Thus, the ratio is B2 B1=N2 N1R1R2=N2 N1×N2 N1

B2 B1=254

subject: Physics
solution:
Energy gained due to potential difference by charged particle:

KE=qV

Relation between momentum & kinetic energy:

p=2mKE

Now, radius of the circular path under magnetic field will be given by, 

R=mvqB=pqB=2m(KE)qB=2mqVqB

Therefore,

R∝m⇒m∝R2

⇒m1 m2=R1R22

 M→=-3.52k^ A m2

=-72k^ A m2

subject: Physics
solution:
First, recall the formula of change in internal energy in terms of molar specific heat at constant volume and change in temperature, ∆U=nCVΔT, for diatomic gas, CV=f2R=52R, ⇒∆U=n×52×RΔT

⇒ΔUW=52nRΔTnRΔT=52=2510

So, x=25

$$

subject: Physics
solution:
Work done by the external force is equal to the change in the potential energy,

Wext=ΔU=Uf-Ui

⇒Wext=-MBcosθ2+MBcosθ1

⇒Wext=MBcosθ1-cosθ2

⇒Wext=14×10-5×2×105cos0°-cos60°

⇒Wext=14×2×12=14 J

subject: Physics
solution:
The direction of the magnetic field at the observation point due to the current carrying conductor is determined by the right-hand cork screw rule.

From the given diagram, it is clear that as the conductors carry current in the mutually opposite direction, the direction of the magnetic fields produced by them at point P will be the same.

Hence, the required magnetic field at P due to the conductors can be calculated as follows:

B=2×μ0i2πa=2×4π×10-7×102π×52×10-2 T=160×10-6 T=160 μT

subject: Physics
solution:
Magnetic flux through a coil is given by ϕ=B→.A→=BAcosθ, where,Bis magnetic field, A is area of coil and θ is the angle between B and A.

Thus, from above expression, we can say that magnetic flux can be changed by changing magnetic field, area of coil, change the angle between B and A.

Hence, correct options are A, B and C.

subject: Physics
solution:
The Lorentz force experienced by a charged particle is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Given that the particle moves in the $x$-$y$ plane, its velocity can be written as $\vec{v} = v_x \hat{i} + v_y \hat{j}$.

Substituting the given values into the force equation:

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} [0.4\hat{j} + (v_x \hat{i} + v_y \hat{j}) \times (4 \times 10^{-3} \hat{k})]$

Using the cross product rules $\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$ and $\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$:

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} [0.4\hat{j} - 4 \times 10^{-3} v_x \hat{j} + 4 \times 10^{-3} v_y \hat{i}]$

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} [4 \times 10^{-3} v_y \hat{i} + (0.4 - 4 \times 10^{-3} v_x) \hat{j}]$

The given force is $\vec{F} = (4\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) \times 10^{-10}$ N, which can be rewritten as:

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} (0.4\hat{i} + 0.2\hat{j})$ N

Comparing the coefficients of $\hat{i}$ and $\hat{j}$ from both expressions:

For the $\hat{i}$ component:
$4 \times 10^{-3} v_y = 0.4$
$v_y = \dfrac{0.4}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 100$ m/s

For the $\hat{j}$ component:
$0.4 - 4 \times 10^{-3} v_x = 0.2$
$4 \times 10^{-3} v_x = 0.2$
$v_x = \dfrac{0.2}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 50$ m/s

Therefore, the velocity of the particle is $\vec{v} = 50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j}$ m/s.

Answer: $50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j}$

solution:
According to Ampere's law,

∮B→·dl→=μ0i

Inside the hollow cylinder, current will be zero. Therefore, magnetic field will also be zero.

Outside the cylinder,

B2πr=μ0i⇒B=μ0i2πr⇒B∝1r

Hence, the correct answer is D.

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic moment of the coil is given by $M = NIA$.

The torque acting on the coil is $\tau = MB \sin\theta = NIAB \sin\theta$.

Substituting the given values:

$\tau = 125 \times 1 \times (\pi \times (0.02)^2) \times 0.4 \times \sin 30^\circ$

$\tau = 125 \times 1 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 \times 0.5$

$\tau = 100\pi \times 10^{-4}$

$\tau = 100 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} = 314 \times 10^{-4}$ N.m

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-4}$ N.m, we get $\alpha = 314$.

Answer: $314$

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field at any axial point situated at a distance x from the centre of a circular current carrying conductor of radius r can be written as

B=μ0Ir22r2+x23/2   ...1

Substitute 0 for x in equation (1) to calculate the magnetic field Bc at the centre of the conductor.

Bc=μ0Ir22r2+023/2= μ0Ir22r3=μ0I2r   ...2

Substitute r for x in equation (1) to calculate the magnetic field Br at the given distance from the centre of the conductor.

Br=μ0Ir22r2+r23/2= μ0Ir242r3=μ0I42r   ...3

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) to calculate the required ratio of the magnetic fields.

BcBr=μ0I2rμ0I42r= 22=8

subject: Physics
solution:
Torque on a bar magnet kept in a magnetic field is given by τ=MBsinθ.

Hence, for small oscillations the time period of bar magnets is given by,

T=2πIMB⇒M∝IT2

⇒M1M2=I1I2×T2T12

⇒M1M2=32×432=83

subject: Physics
solution:
Fiven that E→=Ek^ and B→=12 j^ mT

Kinetic energy=728 eV

Kinetic energy =12mv2

⇒728 eV=12×9.1×10-31×v2

⇒728×1.6×10-19=12×9.1×10-31×v2

⇒v=16×106 m s-1

For electron to move undeflected net force on it should be zero.

⇒eE=evB

⇒E=vB=16×106×12×10-3

⇒E=192×103 V m-1=192 kV m-1
